briefdescription.para: Mathematics argument out of domain of function.
Too many levels of symbolic links.
Too many files open in system.
No message is available on the STREAM head read queue.
No message of the desired type.
Not a directory or a symbolic link to a directory.
Value too large to be stored in data type.
The name could not be resolved at this time.
The flags had an invalid value.
The address family was not recognized or the address length was invalid for the specified family.
There was a memory allocation failure.
The name does not resolve for the supplied parameters.
The service passed was not recognized for the specified socket type.
The intended socket type was not recognized.
The function type of a handler for get_errc().

detaileddescription.para: The default implementation uses GetLastError() on Windows and errno on other platforms.

parameterdescription.para: the extra argument specified to set_errnum_get_handler().

briefdescription.para: The function type of a handler for set_errc().

detaileddescription.para: The default implementation uses SetLastError() on Windows and errno on other platforms.

parameterdescription.para: the most recent error code to be set.
the extra argument specified to set_errnum_set_handler().

briefdescription.para: Retrieves the handler function for get_errc().

parameterdescription.para: the address at which to store a pointer to the handler function (can be NULL).
the address at which to store the pointer to the extra argument for the handler function (can be NULL).

briefdescription.para: Retrieves the handler function for set_errc().

parameterdescription.para: the address at which to store a pointer to the handler function (can be NULL).
the address at which to store the pointer to the extra argument for the handler function (can be NULL).

detaileddescription.para: If set to NULL, get_errc() will always return 0.

parameterdescription.para: handler to be used with get_errc() (can be NULL).
an optional pointer to an extra argument for the handler function (can be NULL).

detaileddescription.para: If set to NULL, set_errc() will not store the error code.

parameterdescription.para: handler to be used with set_errc() (can be NULL).
an optional pointer to an extra argument for the handler function (can be NULL).

briefdescription.para: Transforms a native error code to a platform-independent error number.
Transforms a platform-independent error number to a native error code.
Returns the last native error code set by set_errc() by invoking the handler set by set_errc_get_handler().

detaileddescription.para: The default implementation returns the last (thread-specific) native error code set by a system call or library function. This is equivalent to GetLastError()/WSAGetLastError() on Windows, or errno on other platforms.
This function returns the thread-specific error number.

briefdescription.para: Sets the current native error code to errc by invoking the handler set by set_errc_set_handler().

detaileddescription.para: In case of the default implementation. this is equivalent to SetLastError(errc)/WSASetLastError(errc) on Windows, or errno = errc on other platforms.

briefdescription.para: Returns the native error code to the value corresponding to errno, if available.

detaileddescription.para: This is equivalent to errno2c(errno). If LELY_NO_ERRNO, this functions returns 0.

briefdescription.para: Sets the native error code to the value corresponding to errno, if available.

detaileddescription.para: This is equivalent to set_errc(errno2c(errno)). If LELY_NO_ERRNO is defined, the native error code is set to 0.

briefdescription.para: Returns the last (thread-specific) platform-independent error number set by a system call or library function.

detaileddescription.para: This is equivalent to errc2num(get_errc()) (or get_errc() if LELY_NO_ERRNO is defined).

briefdescription.para: Sets the current (thread-specific) platform-independent error number to errnum.

detaileddescription.para: This is equivalent to set_errc(errnum2c(errnum)) (or set_errnum(errnum) if LELY_NO_ERRNO is defined).

briefdescription.para: This header file is part of the utilities library; it contains the native and platform-independent error number declarations.

detaileddescription.para: The C standard defines the (thread-local) errno variable plus a small number of error numbers (which are guaranteed to be positive). POSIX platforms extend the list of pre-defined error numbers to cover all platform-specific errors. Windows defines a separate (thread-local) variable, accessible by GetLastError()/SetLastError(), for all system errors. A subset of these, accessible by WSAGetLastError()/WSASetLastError(), is used for Windows Sockets errors. On top of this, both POSIX and Windows maintain a list of error codes returned by the getaddrinfo() and getnameinfo() functions. On Windows these error codes have the same values as their corresponding system errors, while on Linux they all have negative values, allowing them to be distinguished from valid errno values. Unfortunately on Cygwin their values are positive and overlap with errno error numbers.

In order to minimize information loss, we would like to keep track of platform-dependent error numbers when storing or propagating errors, but do so in a platform-independent way. get_errc() and set_errc() provide access to the current (thread-local) error code. These functions are equivalent to GetLastError() and SetLastError() on Windows and map to errno on other platforms. Negative errno values are used to store the error codes returned by getaddrinfo() and getnameinfo(), even if the original error codes are positive (e.g., on Cygwin). Since Windows also uses errno (for standard C functions), set_errc(errno2c(errno)) can be used to portably translate errno to a native error code (this is a no-op on POSIX platforms).

When responding to errors it is desirable to have a list of platform-independent error numbers. The errnum_t data type provides the ERRNUM_* error values. These values are guaranteed to be positive, unique and to have the same value across all platforms. There is an ERRNUM_* value for each of the (non-reserved) POSIX error numbers as well as the error codes returned by getaddrinfo() and getnameinfo(). Translating between native and platform-independent error numbers can be done with errc2num() and errnum2c().

If LELY_NO_ERRNO is defined, <errno.h> is not included and errno is not used. Instead, set_errnum() and get_errnum() store and retrieve the platform-independent error number in a static variable (thread-local if supported). This behavior can be overridden with set_errc_set_handler() and get_errc_set_handler().
